coredata.dc:title: The effect of microwave power level and post-synthesis annealing treatment on oxygen-based functional groups present on carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Al Farsi, Basim
Sofin, R.G. Sumesh
Al Shidhani, Hamood
El-Shafey, El-Said I.
Al-Hosni, Abdullah S.
Al Marzouqi, Faisal
Issac, Abey
Al Nabhani, Abdulrahman
Abou-Zied, Osama K.
coredata.dc:description: Despite widespread interest in understanding the properties of carbon quantum dots (CDOTs), there appear to be fewer studies systamaticaly investigating CDOTs' luminescent mechanisms. The primary objective of this study is to prepare CDOTs with desirable optical properties and to understand the photoluminescence (PL) mechanisms involved in order to control the emission charectersitcs. The optical properties of CDOTs are strongly influenced by the functional groups (-OH, –CHO, –COOH, –CO, –COO) formed on the surface of the dots. The key factors affecting the nature and number of functional groups are the preparation method, post synthesis treatment, and the precursor materials. Ascorbic acid was used as the starting material in this study to synthesize CDOTs via microwave-assisted synthesis techniques. Then, the effect of microwave power level and post synthesis annealing temperature on the oxygen-based functional groups present on the surface of the dots was evaluated systematically. It was found that the increasing microwave power level increases the density of functional groups present on the surface of CDOTs, which positively affects the PL. However, annealing at high temperatures decreases the concentration of functional groups and reduces the PL intensity. The PL emission peaks were deconvoluted and the emission wavelength corresponding to each functional group was identified. This work underlines the role of oxygen based functional groups on the PL emission and the ways to control them by physical means.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoluminescence
Functional groups
Microwave synthesis
Carbon quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–d) TEM image of the CDOTs; (e–h) the particle size distribution and (i–l) the lattice spacing distribution of the CDOTs at different power levels (270, 450,720 and 900 W).
(a–d) T.E.M. image of the CDOTs; (e–h) the particle size distribution (i–l) the lattice spacing distribution of the CDOTs at different annealing temperature.
X-Ray diffraction patterns of a. CDOTs at a different power levels and b. CDOTs - 900 Watt sample before and after the annealing treatment under Ar gas.

FT-IR spectra of (a) CDOTs at different power levels, (b) CDOTs −900 W at the various annealing temperatures. (c–d) Surface functionality and pHzpc (e–f) and (g–h) effect of pH on zeta potential of CDOTs at different power levels and CDOTs-900 W at different annealing temperatures, respectively.
Deconvolution of C 1s and O 1s peak: (a–e) 270 W, (b–f) 450 W, (c–g) 720 W, and (d–h) 900 W samples.
Deconvolution of C 1s and O 1s peak: (a–e) 200 °C, (b–f) 400 °C, (c–g) 600 °C, and (d–h) 800 °C samples.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of samples a. prepared at different power levels b. annealed at different temperatures.
a) Room-temperature PL (b–e) deconvoluted spectra f) integrated emission intensity (Area %) of samples prepared at different microwave power levels.
(a) Room-temperature PL Spectra (b–e) deconvoluted spectra (f) integrated emission intensity (Area %) of samples annealed at different temperatures.
a) Emission transients (310 nm excitation) of samples at different power levels. b) Total lifetime of the samples. c) Amplitudes of short and long lifetime components.
Elemental analysis for CDOTs a) at different power levels (270,450,720 and 900 W) and b) CDOTs-900 Watts sample at different annealing temperature (200,400,600 and 800 °C).
Atomic weight percentages of elements in the CDOTs samples prepared at different microwave power levels.
Atomic weight percentages of elements in the CDOTs samples annealed at different temperatures.
Results of deconvoluted emission peaks of CDOTs prepared at different microwave power levels.
Results of deconvoluted emission peaks of CNPs-900 W at different annealing temperatures.

head.ce:title: The effect of microwave power level and post-synthesis annealing treatment on oxygen-based functional groups present on carbon quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite widespread interest in understanding the properties of carbon quantum dots (CDOTs), there appear to be fewer studies systamaticaly investigating CDOTs' luminescent mechanisms. The primary objective of this study is to prepare CDOTs with desirable optical properties and to understand the photoluminescence (PL) mechanisms involved in order to control the emission charectersitcs. The optical properties of CDOTs are strongly influenced by the functional groups (-OH, –CHO, –COOH, –CO, –COO) formed on the surface of the dots. The key factors affecting the nature and number of functional groups are the preparation method, post synthesis treatment, and the precursor materials. Ascorbic acid was used as the starting material in this study to synthesize CDOTs via microwave-assisted synthesis techniques. Then, the effect of microwave power level and post synthesis annealing temperature on the oxygen-based functional groups present on the surface of the dots was evaluated systematically. It was found that the increasing microwave power level increases the density of functional groups present on the surface of CDOTs, which positively affects the PL. However, annealing at high temperatures decreases the concentration of functional groups and reduces the PL intensity. The PL emission peaks were deconvoluted and the emission wavelength corresponding to each functional group was identified. This work underlines the role of oxygen based functional groups on the PL emission and the ways to control them by physical means.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dissemination of hazardous chemicals into the environment needs to be controlled for the health and well-being of the human race. Many such materials need to be removed from the technological applications to sustain the progress. For example, fluorescent materials containing toxic metals such as Cd, Se and Te, etc., cannot be used in biological applications such as medical diagnostics and bioimaging [1–4]. The same applies to other applications like luminescent devices [5,6], chemical sensing [5] and Hg ion sensors [7].

Carbon-based fluorescent quantum dots (CDOTs) are identified as a potential solution to this problem because of their environmental friendliness and biocompatibility [8–16]. The production of CDOTs is simple and inexpensive [12,13]. It forms a stable aqueous solution [17] and has good ionic stability [18]. CDOTs absorb in the ultraviolet region, and the emission is in the visible range with a short lifetime of 1–2 ns [18,19]. Synthesis and application of CDOTs with a fluorescent quantum yield of up to 85% have been reported recently [19].

However, the photoluminescence (PL) mechanism of CDOTs is still under investigation [12,13,20,21]. Various emission models have been proposed, and the following are some suggested mechanisms. a) Contributions from surface defects/traps, b) bandgap transitions contributing to the presence of sp2-nanodomains, c) the molecular state defined by luminescent molecules attached to the surface or inside the CDOTs, and d) crosslink enhanced emission [13,22,23]. Radiative transitions of photo-excited electrons at the core of CDOTs are also reported [20,21]. However, a recent report suggests that the gap originating from the carbon core is negligible and isolated sp2 clusters on the surface of dots can have a HOMO-LUMO gap (3 eV) similar to pyridine derivative units [24]. A new study by Basim et al. has demonstrated the role of functional groups such as carbonyl, carboxylic, phenol, lactone and isolated sp2 clusters in the emission mechanism of activated carbon dots [13]. The proposed model suggested that photoexcited electrons from sp2 clusters can populate mid-emissive levels of functional groups. Radiative transitions from these levels to the ground state contribute a significant part of the emission spectrum. There are many reports about the role of functional groups in the emission mechanism [6,24–28]. It has been shown that functional groups with a CO are favourable for tuning the optical and electrical properties of CDOTs [6,29,30]. A theoretical study by Geethalakshimi et al. proposed a technique for altering the optical gap by geometrical distortion of functional groups on the surface of CDOTs [31]. The hydroxyl groups attached to the edges of sp2 conjugated domains of CDOTs can increase the QY, while carboxyl groups give the opposite result [35]. This is because carboxyl groups can act as non-radiative recombination centres. The number and types of functional groups present on the surface of CDOTs depend on the synthesis method and post-synthesis treatment.

Different methods for the synthesis of carbon nanoparticles have been reported, including ultrasound- and laser-based methods [38,39], hydrothermal methods [40], electrochemical oxidation [41], arc discharge [42], chemical methods [43], and microwave-assisted methods [32,44,45]. Microwave-assisted Synthesis (MAS) produces a very rich functionalized surface among these methods. The one-step MAS is simple to use, inexpensive, and fast [33]. Carboxyl, lactone, phenol, and carbonyl are important functional groups found on the CDOTs surface. By controlling the functionalization steps, it is possible to achieve the desired number of specific functional groups on the surface of CDOTs [13,34,35]. The microwave power level used during the synthesis plays a crucial role in forming functional groups on the particle surface and, therefore, the emission characteristics [36–38]. To the best of the authors' knowledge, no detailed studies have been published on the effect of microwave power levels during the synthesis of CDOTs concerning the amount of specific functional groups formed on the surface. Moreover, the post-synthesis annealing of samples can control the density of different functional groups. This work focuses on emission characteristics and how the functional groups on the surface of CDOTs, are affected by the microwave power level during the synthesis and the post-synthesis annealing treatment. The work is significant because it provides a fascinating insight into the physical ways to control oxygen-based functional groups of CDOTs, linking with investigations to assess the role of distinct functional groups in the PL emission mechanism.
The base material for the Synthesis of CDOTs is ascorbic acid (C6H8O6) (Sigma Aldrich with high purity (over 99%).). This is because it is an abundant and simple organic acid, like citric acid [22].

The microwave-assisted synthesis method was used for the preparation of CDOTs samples. A magnetic stirrer was used to dissolve 7.1 g of ascorbic acid in 25 mL of deionized water until it resulted in a saturated solution. The saturated solution was heated in a microwave oven under different microwave power levels (270, 450, 720, and 900 W) for 8min. The produced black powder was cooled down at room temperature and crushed using a mortar and pestle to powder form. These samples are labeled as CDOTs −270 W, CDOTs −450 W, CDOTs −720 W and CDOTs −900 W. For the annealing studies, CDOTs −900 W samples are placed inside a tube furnace (inside diameter 4 cm and 60 cm long) and heated at 200, 400, 600, and 800 °C for 60 min under argon (Ar) gas pressure of 2.4 × 10 −2 mbar. The temperature ramping rate was 15 °C/min and after annealing samples were cooled overnight. These samples are labeled as CDOTs −200C, CDOTs −400C, CDOTs −600C and CDOTs −800 °C.

All the samples were washed and filtered several times using DI water. After washing, 15 g of the powder sample was dispersed in 100 mL of DI water, then the suspension was allowed to settle, and the top layer was collected. This was followed by centrifugation, and again, the top layer was collected and dried at ambient conditions. These processes were continued until the desired amount of particles were obtained. A schematic of the preparation steps is presented in Fig. 1 .

Philips X-ray diffractometer (model PW 1820) was used for the structural characterization, and a JEOL (Model: JEM 1400) transmission electron microscope (TEM) was employed to check the lattice and morphology of the samples. The images were analyzed using ImageJ software. Surface analysis was done using Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX: Jeol JSM-7800 F), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Omicron Nanotechnology, Germany) with (Al) Kα (hν = 1486.6 eV) radiated at 15 kV. The XPS results were analyzed by Casa XPS software (Casa Software Ltd, UK). The infrared absorption spectroscopy was carried out by an FT-IR spectrometer (PerkinElmer, Spectrum One, USA) through a wavenumber ranging from 4000 to 400 cm−1 with a signal resolution of 4 cm−1 with 40 scans. 0.005 g of carbon dots were mixed with 0.995 g of potassium bromide (KBr) and then pressed under high pressure (10 tons) to form compact pellets. These pellets were used for the FT-IR measurements.

The Elemental analysis was carried out using a CHNS/O analyzer (Euro EA 3000 elemental analyzer, UK). Zeta potential as a function of pH was measured using the SurPASSTM electrokinetic analyzer (UK). The pH of the solution was adjusted with 0.01 M NaOH and 0.01 M HCl during the experiment. UV–Visible absorption and photoluminescence were measured using Agilent 8453 and Shimadzu RF–5301spectrometers. Transient measurements were carried out using a fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (TimeMaster, Photon Technology International).

Boehm titrations procedure was used to quantify the density of functional groups (lactone, phenol, carboxyl, and carbonyl) on the surface of CDOTs utilizing selective neutralization capacity [39]. In 30 mL polyethylene bottles, 0.125 g of each sample was mixed with 25 mL standard solutions of 0.1 M NaHCO3, 0.1 M NaOH, 0.1 M NaOEt, and 0.05 M Na2CO3 for carboxyl, phenol, carbonyl, and lactone, respectively. To prevent the base-catalyzed auto-oxidation of carbon in the presence of oxygen, nitrogen (N2) was flushed through each sample for 5 min [40]. To achieve equilibrium, the samples were left in a shaking water bath for two days (48 h) at room temperature. The fall in each solution's concentration was assessed by titrating all aliquots against standardized 0.1 M HCl using methyl orange as an indicator.

Fig. 2 (a–d) shows the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of carbon dots (CDOTs) prepared at different power levels (270, 450, 720 and 900 W). TEM images show a narrow size distribution and mono-dispersion with spherical morphology in aqueous media. The average dot size is affected by the microwave power level and the annealing temperature. For example, for the increasing the microwave power levels (270, 450, 720, and 900 W), the average size of the dots is found to decrease (35.5
± 3
, 3.6
± 0.8
, 3.3
± 0.9
, and 2.2
± 0.5
nm, respectively) (Fig. 2e–h). The decreasing dot size with increasing power levels can be attributed to a higher density of nucleation sites at higher power levels [41]. Increasing the annealing temperature also decreases the average dot size distribution, as shown in Fig. 3 . We found 2.40
± 0.2
nm at 200 °C, 2.2
7 ± 0.1
nm at 400 °C,1.44
± 0.1
nm at 600 °C and 0.73
± 0.04
nm at 800 °C. The annealing treatment can cause a self-etching process for the carbon particles resulting in smaller dots [42]. Figs. 2 and 3 (i–l) show the presence of atomic lattice in the dots. The average lattice spacing from the distribution was found to be around 0.33
± 0.04
, 0.24
± 0.09
, 0.28
± 0.04
, and 0.32
± 0.04
nm, which correspond to (002) diffraction facets of graphite [2,24]. This may be attributed to the sp2 (1120) graphitic crystal phase of graphene [43]. As the power level increased, the lattice spacing approached a value close to the value of graphite (0.34 nm) [44].

The XRD spectra of CDOTs at different power levels are shown in Fig. 4 a. All the samples show a broad peak centred around 19°(near to (002) reflection of graphite (JCPDS Card Files, No. 41–1487) [54]) with an interlayer spacing of 0.46 nm, slightly larger than that of graphite (0.34 nm). The peak broadness (The FWHM was found to be 10.35, 11.9, 11.1, and 12.2°.) could be due to the large distribution of the lattice spacing [13]. Fig. 4 b shows the XRD pattern of CDOTs-900 W at different annealing temperatures. The (002) peak at 19° is shifted to 23.2°, corresponding to a change in the distribution of lattice spacing. The annealing treatment causes the lattice spacing to change from 0.46 nm to 0.38 nm, which is closer to the lattice spacing of graphite (0.34 nm) [44]. Also, the weak and broad C (100) diffraction peak (2θ = 43°) is due to the a-axis of the graphite structure [45,46]. It is observed at 800 °C and 600 °C, which are related to graphitic carbon with lattice spacing 0.21 nm [43] (JCPDS Card Files, No. 41–1487) [44]. However, this trend was not evident in the TEM results. This is because XRD results give the global average of the lattice distribution compared to TEM where the sampling size is limited.

The FTIR spectrum of ascorbic acid and the carbon dots prepared at different power levels are shown in Fig. 5.a. In general, the FTIR spectra of CDOTs have absorption bands at 3000–3640, 2918, 2848, 1700, 1400, and 1000 cm−1 attributed to vibrations OH stretching, stretching C–H vibrations in CH3 CH2 groups, carboxylic group CO, CC, and (C–H or C–O) bending, respectively [13,34,47]. The spectrum indicates an increasing density of functional groups with increasing power levels. However, the samples prepared at higher microwave power levels (CDOTs-900w) show more vibrational peaks (CO), which are characteristics of functional groups present on the surface of CDOTs. As illustrated in Fig. 5 b, annealing causes the functional groups to disappear from the surface of the dots. The CC band indicates that the CDOTs are graphitic in structure, while the OH, CO, and C–H bending bands suggest that the CDOTs have OH, CO, and C–H surface moieties attached to the surface [48,49]. The values for surface functionalization (meq/g) of 270, 450, 720, and 900 W samples are shown in Fig. 5 c. As the power level increased the functionalization of carboxylic, phenol and lactone groups increased by 250%, 34% and 96% respectively. However, functionalization of carbonyl group was decreased by 38%. Overall the functionalization increases as the power level increases. The post sytheysis annealing decreases the functionalization indicating the removal of functional groups from the surface of CDOTs (see Fig. 5d). However, the ratio between functionalization of different groups at each annealing temperature is not affected much. pHzpc decreases as the synthesis power level increases and negative charge of the dots increases as the pH of the solution increases (see Fig. 5 e and g). This suggests that number of carboxylic groups on the surface of the dots are increasing with power level and deprotonation of carboxylic groups increases as the pH of the solution increases. This argument is supported by the results of annealed samples. Fig. 5 f and h shows higher values of pHzpc compared to unanneald samples and it increase (pH 4.70 (900 W-200 °C) to pH 5.98 (900 W-800 °C)) as the annealing temperature increases. This is because the number of functional groups are decreasing as the annealing temperature increases [34,35]. It is clear from the studies that the density of various functional groups on the surface of CDOTs depends on the synthesis power levels and post-annealing treatment.

Results of CHN and EDX are given in Table .1 . The H/C and O/C ratios increased as the power level increased. It has been reported earlier that direct absorption of microwaves by solvents such as water can increase the reaction rate of the dissolved reagents [50]. This is because of the high dielectric constant of the water molecules which strongly interact with the electric field of microwaves. This strong interaction elevates the temperature of the system and accelerates the reactions. Conventional heating is different from microwave heating. Microwave heating involves the rotation of molecules and dipolar polarization which produces explicit effects which could not be achieved by conventional heat treatments [50]. This could be the reason for higher H/C and O/C ratios at higher microwave power levels. H/C and O/C ratios were found to decrease as the annealing temperature increased. This could be due to the loss of hydrogen and oxygen due to a higher rate of aromatization and decarboxylation reactions, respectively, during the carbonization process [63,64]. Dehydration is another factor that contributes to the decreasing H/C and O/C ratios [62, 63]. The change mentioned above is more pronounced in annealed samples than in samples with varying power levels. By estimating the mass to weight ratio before and after synthesis, we determined that the sample yield was lower at higher annealing temperatures and power levels (see Table 1).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to perform the elemental analysis, and the results are shown in Fig. S2. The two peaks, detected at the binding energies, 284 eV and 532 eV, are assigned to C 1s and O 1s, respectively [51]. Estimated carbon and oxygen content in the samples are listed in Tables 2 and 3
. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s of the samples at different power levels are shown in Fig. 6 a–d. C1s spectra were deconvoluted into four individual component peaks at 285.1eV, 286.9 eV, 288.9 eV, and 291.6eV, and these peaks are attributed to C–C, C–O, CO, and OC–O bonds, respectively [52]. O 1s spectra of the sample prepared at 270 W is shown in (Fig. 6 e-h.) indicate two characteristic peaks to C–O at 531.6eV and CO at 532.5 [52,53]. However, three peaks, C–O (284.8 eV), CO (285.7 eV), and COO- at 535.8 are observed in the case of samples prepared at 450 W, 720 W, and 900 W [54].

In the case of annealed samples, C1s spectrum of the sample annealed at 200 °C (Fig. 7 ) could be deconvoluted into five peaks. These peaks correspond to CC (284.8 eV), C–C (285.7 eV), C–O (287.3 eV), and CO (288.2 eV), and π-π* (292.9 eV) [54], indicating the existence of oxygen-containing groups as in the unannealed sample. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 7) of CDOTs at annealed temperatures 400 °C, 600 °C, and 800 °C also indicated three peaks at 284.1 eV, 285.6 eV, and 288 eV, which are attributed to CC, C-Cand C–O, respectively. Fig. 7 e-h shows high-resolution O 1s spectra of the sample annealed at 200 °C.

It can be noticed that the spectrum can be separated into three characteristic peaks. These are, C–O at 531.6eV, CO at 532.5, and COO- at 535.8 [54]. The two peaks C–O (284.8 eV) and CO (285.7 eV) are found in samples annealed at 400 °C, 600 °C, and 800 °C [52,53].

Fig. 8 a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDOTs in DI water. The sharp peak at 254 nm, corresponds to n- π* transition of the CO [55]. The shoulder peak observed at 296 nm can be assigned to n- π* transition of non-conjugated electron orbitals originating from carboxyl (CO) bonds on the surface of CDOTs [56–59]. The FWHM was found to be 32.61, 45.11, 46.71 and 48.91 nm when the power level increased as shown in Fig. 8 c. It can be noted that the increasing peak broadness (with increasing power level) is due to the increasing oxygen functional groups on the surface on CDOTs. Fig. 8 b shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDOTs-900 W sample (in water) annealed at different temperatures. As annealing temperature increases the absorption peaks at 254 nm and 296 nm were found to decrease suggesting the reduction of functional groups on the surface of CDOTs due to annealing. UV–Vis result suggests that the annealing of CDOTs has a significant effect on the nature and occurrence of functional groups on the surface of CDOTs.

Room-temperature emission spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, which is well below the absorption wavelength cutoff (500 nm) of CDOTs. The excitation spectrum shows maximum emission when excited at 350 nm. Fig. 9 a shows the PL intensity plots of dots at different microwave power levels. Deconvolution of peaks was done using multi-Gaussian peak fitting routine to identify the emission source. Each peak was deconvoluted into four sub-peaks. The results are summarized in Table 4 .

Results indicate that while the peak position remains constant with increasing power levels, the total area and deconvoluted peak areas increase. The area represents the total energy emitted. As discussed earlier, the density of functional groups increases as the microwave power level increases. Therefore the emission is mainly form the functional groups and increasing area of the peaks suggest the increasing density of functional groups [12,13].

In our previous publication we have identified the emissions corresponding to different functional groups (such as carbonyl, carboxylic, phenol and lactone) as well as isolated
s p
2
clusters on the surface on activated carbon particles prepared by a green synthesis method [12,13]. It was also shown that emission from the core of the particles is insignificant [12,13,24]. Four deconvoluted peaks shown in Fig. 9b-e represent four types of emission processes. Based on our previous study we can identify the source of these peaks [12,13]. Peak 1 (3.06 eV energy) could be linked to isolated sp2 clusters on the CDOTs' surface. This is because Fang et al. found that isolated sp2 clusters on the surface of carbon dots exhibit a HOMO-LUMO gap of roughly 3 eV, which is similar to pyridine derivative units [24,25]. The carboxylic functional groups may be responsible for Peak 2 (2.90 eV) [12,13]. The integrated intensity (in percentage) of this peak increases from 26 to 36% as the microwave power level increases (see Fig. 9 f). This result suggest that the density of carboxylic functional groups are increasing with microwave power level. Peak 3 (2.72eV) and Peak 4 (2.50 eV) can be attributed to carbonyl and phenol groups as reported before [12,13,24,25]. The integrated area of Peak 3 decreases from 40 to 28% (see Fig. 9 f) suggesting decreasing density of carbonyl groups on the surface whereas it increases from 14 to 26% in the case of Peak 4 of phenol group. All these results are in line with our surface functionalization results discussed earlier. Therefore, our PL results suggest that emission from CDOTs is mainly due to functional groups such as phenolic, carbonyl, and carboxylic groups. The density of these groups on the surface is affected by the microwave power level during the synthesis. The possible mechanism could be the following. photoexcited electrons from sp2 clusters ccould populate mid level emissive states orginating from different functional groups. Radiative transitions from these levels to the ground state could be contributing to different sub peaks of the emission spectram. Fig. 10 a and Table 5 show PL spectra of annealed samples. The total area of the peak was found to decrease after annealing (see Fig. 10 f). This suggests the reduction of density of functional groups due to annealing. This is again in line with the surface functionality results. Deconvolutions of the peaks resulted in five sub peaks (Fig. 10 b-e). Apart from the first four peaks an additional peak at the low energy side (2.38 eV) could be due to the laconic groups [13].

Fig. 11 a shows the emission transients of samples prepared at different microwave power levels. Lifetime was measured by exciting at 310 nm and the decay transients were analyzed with Global Lifetime software. There is a short-lived component τ1 and a long-lived component τ2 in all of the samples. Fig. 11b shows the amplitude weighted total lifetime, which increases from 2.0 to 2.35 ns as the power level increases from 270 W to 900 W. The amplitude associated with τ1 is decreasing with power level, whereas the amplitude associated with τ2 is increasing (Fig. 11c). The overall rise in radiative transitions from midlevel surface states formed by functional groups is shown by the increased amplitude of the long-lived component. This finding is also in line with the previous part of the discussion. As a result, it can be indicated that oxygen-based functional groups on the surface of CDOTs play a main role in the emission process. The surface functionality of the required groups may be controlled by physical means like synthesis power level or annealing treatment to tune emission characteristics.

CDOTs were synthesized using a microwave-assisted method from a saturated ascorbic acid solution. The effect of two parameters (microwave power level and post-synthesis annealing treatment) on the oxygen-based functional groups present on the surface of CDOTs were studied systematically. Different power levels (270 W, 450 W, 720 W, and 900 W) were used during the synthesis, as well as different annealing temperatures (200, 400, 600, and 800 °C, under Ar gas) for post synthesis treatment. The role of functional groups in the PL emission is also studied. As the microwave power level increased the surface functionalization was found to increase in the case of functional groups such as carboxylic, phenol and lactone. However, carbonyl group was found to decrease. The annealing treatment reduced the functionalization in general. Deconvoluted PL spectra was used to identify the role of each functional groups in the emission process. The origin highest energy peak (3.06 eV) was attributed to isolated sp2 clusters on the surface of the dots. Other PL peaks in the decreasing order of energy were assigned to carboxylic (2.90 eV), carbonyl (2.72 eV) and phenol (2.50 eV) groups. The PL emission was found decrease because of the annealing treatment. This is attributed to the reduction of density of functional groups due to the heat treatment. An additional peak (2.38 eV) which could be from lactone group was found after the heat treatment. The results demonstrate that higher microwave power output is more effective at synthesizing CDOTs than lower microwave power output, owing to the system's rapid energy delivery. The results demonstrate that higher microwave power output is more effective at synthesizing CDOTs than lower microwave power output, owing to the system's rapid energy delivery. This work suggests different ways to tune the PL emission characteristics by controlling the number of functional groups by physical means.

BAF, RGS, AI, ES and AAN were involved in the preparation and characterization of the samples. BAF, RGS, AI, HAS, ES, OAZ, AAH, FAM contributed to the writing of the manuscript. All authors discussed and commented on the manuscript. RGS and OAZ secured the required funding for the project.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from Sultan Qaboos University within the framework of research Internal Grant (IG/SCI/PHYS/18/05) and His Majesty's Trust Fund for Strategic Research (SR/SCI/CHEM/18/01). Further, we thank the central analytical and applied research unit at Sultan Qaboos University for the XRD measurements. Prof. Salim Al-Harthi and Dr. Myo Myint for XPS measurements.
